              Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
          Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
          Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
          ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
          Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
          of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
           Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
          ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
           Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
          operative statistical programs of the BLS with
          these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
          Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
          back cover.                                                                        CALENDAR OF SPECIAL FEATURES

                        Employment and Earnings
                        Vol. 25 No. 10 October 1978
                        Editors: Chester L. Green, Gloria P. Green, Rosalie K. Epstein




                                    Editor's Note

                                        Beginning with this issue, establishment-based data (national series) have been revised to conform
                                    to the industry definitions of the 1972 Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) Manual and to reflect
                                    employment benchmark levels (comprehensive counts of payroll employment) for March 1977. In
                                    addition, all seasonally adjusted data have been revised, based on experience through May 1978.
                                    For a discussion of the effect of these revisions, see the article, "BLS Establishment Estimates Revised
                                    to Reflect New Benchmark Levels and 1972 SIC," that appears on page 8. New seasonal factors for
                                    use in current adjustment and historical series of revised seasonally adjusted data from the earliest
                                    dates of availability are shown on pages 180-247.
                                        Historical data in available industry detail (not seasonally adjusted) for 1972 through June 1978
                                    may be obtained on microfiche (at $4.55 per set) from the National Technical Information Service,
                                    U.S. Department of Commerce, 5285 Port Royal Road, Springfield, Virginia, 2 2 1 6 1 . When ordering
                                    please specify Employment and Earnings, United States, 1972-78, BLS Bulletin 1312-1 K M ) ,
                                     PB 285565.

                              BLS Establishment Estimates Revised to Reflect
                                  New Benchmark Levels and 1972 SIC
                                                                                                                            * Michael Buso and
                                                                                                                         William C. Bennett, Jr.

                   The establishment-based national employment esti-         most detailed industrial classification for which estimates
               mates appearing monthly in this publication have been         are made. The difference between the benchmark and the
               revised to new levels to reflect more complete counts of      corresponding estimate is assumed to have accumulated in
               employment, called benchmarks. Also, the series have been     constant increments over the previous 12 months. (See
               converted to reflect the industry definitions published in    discussion below under "Why estimates differ from bench-
               the 1972 Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) Man-        marks.") Most series, therefore, are adjusted by wedging or
               ual. l This marks the first complete benchmark revision       tapering out the difference over the period from the new
               since the March 1974 benchmark levels were introduced         benchmark to the preceding one, 12 months earlier. The
               in October 1975, and the first major industry reclassifica-   benchmark level is then projected forward to the current
               tion since the 1957 SIC was introduced in 1961. 2 New         month based on the trend of monthly reports submitted
               stratification patterns for the estimation procedure have     by a sample of employers. The estimates adjusted to the
               also been introduced, both for compatibility with the re-     new levels are then aggregated through successively larger
               vised SIC and for improved accuracy in estimation. This       groupings to total nonagricultural employment.
               article reviews the effects of the latest (March 1977)
               benchmarks on the employment estimates and discusses the      Conversion to 1972 SIC
               conversion of the national industry series to the 1972 SIC
                                                                                The SIC system is revised periodically to reflect the
               system.
                                                                             changing industrial composition of the economy, although
                   New benchmarks were last introduced to the national
                                                                             efforts are made to maintain comparability with estimates
               establishment-based estimates in October 1975, when the
                                                                             produced under earlier classification systems. The 1972
               estimates were revised to agree with complete employment
                                                                             SIC system is the culmination of several years of work by
               counts for March 1974. That revision, however, was based
                                                                             industry specialists in both the private and public sectors.
               on the 1967 SiC system. The benchmark revisions intro-
                                                                                With this issue, the BLS has begun the publication of
               duced in this issue are based on the 1972 SIC system. The
                                                                             national estimates tabulated under the 1972 SIC system.
               individual establishment reports used to generate the esti-

                                    Seasonal Factors




Digitized for FRASER

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
            ESTABLISHMENT DATA
                                                                                                  Seasonal Factors

                  The following tables present seasonal adjustment factors for all series in the establishment section of this periodical. These factors were derived using data through May 1978. The factors should be used
            w i t h current estimates (adjusted to the March 1977 benchmarks) beginning July 1978 for all series shown in the following tables.
               The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model change-
            over periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month change in employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover. The seasonal factors which follow
            enable the analyst to remove these influences from the data in order to determine more basic trends.

                                                                     Explanatory Notes

                                 These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                 (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                 lished in Employment and Earnings.




                                                                             Introduction

                  The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
               sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.

               COLLECTION                                                                   The data exclude proprietors, the self-employed, unpaid volunteer
                                                                                         or family workers, farm workers, and domestic workers in house-
                   Payroll reports provide current information on wage and salary        holds. Salaried officers of corporations are included. Government
               employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover in nonagricultural        employment covers only civilian employees, military person nef are
               establishments, by industry and geographic location.                      excluded. Employees of the Central Intelligence and National

                   One measure of the reliability of the employment estimates
               for individual industries is the root-mean-square error (RMSE).            Table IM. Root-mean-square errors of differences between
               The measure is the standard deviation adjusted for the bias in             benchmarks and estimates of employment and average
